Title: The CYP71A, NIT, AMI, and IAMH gene families are dispensable for indole-3-acetaldoxime-mediated auxin biosynthesis in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not a direct engineering target for nutrient enhancement; useful as a cautionary marker showing that blocking this transporter alters auxin responses to exogenous auxin and IAOx/IAN/IAM-derived IAA transport rather than biosynthetic flux. Relevant for interpreting hormone-transport confounders in metabolic engineering.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AUX1 loss causes partial insensitivity to exogenous IAA, IAOx, IAN, and IAM, indicating that cellular import and movement of IAA contribute to the observed auxin-like responses of these compounds.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping under exogenous IAA/IAOx/IAN/IAM treatments; root and hypocotyl growth assays; DR5:GFP auxin response interpretation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light or darkness; exogenous IAA, IAOx, IAN, IAM treatments.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted metabolomics by LC-MS/MS; single-cell gene expression analysis referenced in Supplementary Fig. S15 without accession provided.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a transport-control comparator, not as a biosynthetic enzyme. Supports that exogenous precursor phenotypes depend partly on IAA transport.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Auxin transport defects can broadly affect development and tropic responses.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Auxin influx carrier, AUX/LAX family transporter
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aux1-7 was partially insensitive to exogenous IAA, IAOx, IAN, and IAM. 2. This indicates that the auxin-like responses from these compounds depend in part on IAA import/cell-to-cell movement. 3. AUX1 functions as a physiological control supporting conversion of exogenous precursors into active IAA rather than defining endogenous IAOx-pathway biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Whole seedling, especially roots and hypocotyls assessed by auxin responses
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulates plant development and environmental responses, including root growth, hypocotyl elongation, tropisms, and organ patterning.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful genetic tool to elevate endogenous IAOx and auxin-related metabolites for pathway discovery. Not suitable as a clean engineering target because sur2 causes severe high-auxin pleiotropy.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SUR2 loss causes auxin overproduction phenotypes and massive IAOx accumulation, but this excess auxin does not require CYP71A, NIT, AMI, or IAMH families tested here.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; genetic interaction with cyp79b2 cyp79b3, cyp71a, nit, ami, iamh mutants; targeted metabolite profiling by LC-MS/MS.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Standard laboratory growth, dark-to-light transition, continuous light, long-day conditions.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted metabolomics by LC-MS/MS; single-cell gene expression analysis referenced in Supplementary Fig. S15 without accession provided.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SUR2 blocks flow of IAOx into indole glucosinolate biosynthesis, genetically activating an alternative auxin-producing route. The study concludes this route is not the classic linear IAOx→IAN→IAM→IAA pathway.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong high-auxin pleiotropy: short roots, altered hypocotyls, epinastic cotyledons/leaves, adventitious rooting, hypocotyl disintegration.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome P450 involved in indole glucosinolate metabolism
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum/microsomal P450 (expected; not directly tested here)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. sur2 accumulated very high IAOx and showed high-auxin phenotypes. 2. Knockout of CYP71A, NIT, AMI/TOC64/FAAH, or IAMH genes did not suppress sur2, whereas cyp79b2 cyp79b3 did. 3. Excess auxin production in sur2 likely uses an unknown IAOx-dependent route rather than the proposed IAOx→IAN→IAM→IAA sequence.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seedlings/rosettes; auxin-related changes across roots and shoots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls plant growth and responses to environment.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Central auxin-biosynthetic control and strong positive control for auxin-deficient phenotypes; highly relevant for editing or stacking to modulate growth, but pleiotropic penalties are likely.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: WEI8/TAA1 deficiency causes mild auxin-deficient phenotypes, reduced ACC response, altered gravitropism, fewer lateral roots, and reduced high-temperature hypocotyl elongation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping across auxin-sensitive assays; genetic interaction with sur2.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ACC treatment, dark/light regimes, high temperature, gravity response assays.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as a benchmark auxin-biosynthesis mutant demonstrating assay sensitivity; belongs to the predominant IPyA pathway rather than the disputed IAOx route.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced auxin biosynthesis impairs growth responses and developmental plasticity.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol (expected for TAA family; not directly tested here)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. wei8 displayed subtle but reproducible auxin-deficient phenotypes in multiple sensitive assays. 2. sur2 partially rescued several wei8 defects, supporting independent auxin-producing inputs. 3. WEI8 serves as a positive control showing that the IAOx-family knockouts lacked comparable endogenous auxin defects.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Whole seedling, especially root and hypocotyl developmental zones
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulates development, tropisms, temperature and ethylene responses.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream-entry control to manipulate IAOx formation in Brassicaceae; however it affects defense-related indole metabolism and auxin homeostasis jointly, so deployment would require balancing growth-defense tradeoffs.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CYP79B2/CYP79B3 are required to produce IAOx upstream of the disputed pathway; cyp79b2 cyp79b3 suppresses sur2 high-auxin phenotypes, confirming IAOx as the relevant upstream intermediate.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double/triple mutant phenotyping with sur2; prior metabolic evidence cited; targeted metabolomics context.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Standard laboratory growth conditions and auxin-sensitive phenotypic assays.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Serves as decisive genetic evidence that excess auxin in sur2 still depends on IAOx formation, even though downstream steps are unknown.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely affects indole glucosinolate/camalexin branches and defense metabolism in addition to auxin-related traits.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum/microsomal P450 (expected; not directly tested here)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cyp79b2 cyp79b3 blocks the formation of IAOx from tryptophan. 2. This mutant suppressed sur2 high-auxin phenotypes under all tested conditions. 3. Therefore, the unknown sur2 auxin-overproduction route likely still requires IAOx as an upstream intermediate.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Whole seedling/plant via systemic auxin homeostasis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls plant development and environmental responses.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Proposed IAOx-dependent auxin biosynthesis / nitrile conversion branch
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tool only for exogenous IAN-to-IAA bioconversion studies; not supported as a target for endogenous IAA bioengineering under normal conditions. Manipulation could alter defense-related nitrile metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NIT family activity is required for converting exogenous IAN into IAA, but NIT1-NIT4 are dispensable for normal endogenous auxin biosynthesis and for sur2-mediated auxin overproduction.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: High-order nit1 nit2 nit3 nit4 mutant; precursor feeding assays with IAN/IAOx/IAM/IAA; phenotyping; sur2 genetic interaction; LC-MS/MS metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product from exogenous precursor; not established as major endogenous source under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light or darkness; exogenous IAN, IAOx, IAA treatments; sur2 background.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not characterized in this paper; single-cell overlap with SUR2 partly referenced in cortex cells.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream side-route from IAN to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted metabolomics by LC-MS/MS; single-cell gene expression analysis referenced in Supplementary Fig. S15 without accession provided.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: nit1234 retained normal response to IAOx, arguing that IAN is not an obligatory intermediate in IAOx-to-IAA conversion. The family is therefore genetically supported for exogenous IAN utilization but not for the canonical endogenous IAOx pathway proposed previously.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb nitrile/defense metabolism; endogenous developmental defects were not prominent under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established for endogenous auxin biosynthesis; essential for exogenous IAN conversion in seedlings.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. nit1234 showed strong insensitivity to exogenous IAN, confirming NIT-dependent IAN-to-IAA conversion. 2. nit1234 responded normally to IAOx and did not suppress sur2. 3. Therefore, NITs are dispensable for the proposed endogenous linear IAOx→IAN→IAM→IAA route under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seedling roots and shoots assessed via growth and DR5 reporter responses
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulates growth, tropisms, and developmental plasticity.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Poor candidate for enhancing endogenous auxin biosynthesis based on this work; AMI-family editing is more useful to exclude false-positive IAM-to-IAA assignments in pathway engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Previously proposed amidase-like hydrolysis of indole-3-acetamide (IAM) to indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), but prominent in vivo contribution is not supported here.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AMI1/TOC64/FAAH family mutants do not show IAM insensitivity, do not alter endogenous auxin phenotypes, and do not suppress sur2, indicating they are dispensable for major IAM-to-IAA conversion in vivo under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: High-order family mutants, including ami1 and toc64/faah combinations; IAM feeding phenotyping; sur2 genetic interaction; RT-qPCR of intronic alleles; LC-MS/MS metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Previously proposed direct formation of target product from IAM, but not supported as a prominent route here.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light or darkness; exogenous IAM, IAOx, IAN, IAA treatments; standard laboratory growth.
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Amidase-like proteins / fatty acid amide hydrolase-related proteins (proposed IAM hydrolases)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not characterized in this paper; single-cell overlap with SUR2 partly referenced.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Name: AMI1; TOC64-III; TOC64-V; FAAH1; FAAH2; FAAH3; FAAH4
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream side-route from IAM to IAA (proposed)
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted metabolomics by LC-MS/MS; RT-qPCR expression analysis of mutant alleles.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: faah3 homozygous loss was embryo lethal. Residual FAAH1/FAAH4 expression was considered but judged unlikely to explain the lack of phenotype. Data contradict a prominent IAM-to-IAA role for this family in vivo.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: faah3 embryo lethality; possible unrelated roles in fatty acid amide metabolism and chloroplast protein import for TOC64 proteins.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Proposed IAA, but not demonstrated as major in vivo product in this study
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AMI1 amidase-related protein; TOC64 proteins; FAAH family amidase/hydrolase-related proteins
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; evidence argues against a major control step in IAA production.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Various, including chloroplast outer membrane association for TOC64 and likely cytosolic/ER-associated hydrolases for FAAHs; not directly tested here
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. High-order AMI-family mutants lacked prominent auxin-deficient phenotypes. 2. These mutants responded normally to exogenous IAM, unlike iamh1 iamh2. 3. AMI1/TOC64/FAAH genes are dispensable for sur2 excess auxin production and are unlikely to be major IAM-to-IAA enzymes in vivo.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seedling tissues, especially hypocotyl and root growth-response zones
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulates plant growth and environmental responses.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: More credible enzymatic entry point for IAM-to-IAA conversion than AMI1 in Arabidopsis seedlings; can be used to modulate sensitivity to IAM-derived auxin, though not sufficient to alter sur2-driven auxin overproduction.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes indole-3-acetamide (IAM) to indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) in vivo.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: IAMH1/IAMH2 are required for normal hypocotyl elongation response to exogenous IAM but are dispensable for responses to IAOx or IAN and for auxin overproduction in sur2.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: iamh1 iamh2 double mutant and iamh1 iamh2 sur2 triple mutant phenotyping under IAM/IAOx/IAN/IAA treatments.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product from exogenous precursor.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light and dark assays; exogenous IAM, IAOx, IAN, IAA; standard growth conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream side-route from IAM to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Phenotypic assays only in this study context; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Normal sensitivity of iamh1 iamh2 to IAOx and IAN argues IAM is not an obligate intermediate in IAOx- or IAN-derived IAA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May specifically affect responses to IAM-containing environments or microbial IAM sources; no suppression of sur2 means limited impact on endogenous overproduction route.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key for exogenous IAM utilization in seedlings; not established as major endogenous control point.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. iamh1 iamh2 showed clear resistance to exogenous IAM. 2. The mutant responded normally to IAOx and IAN, indicating IAM is not a necessary intermediate downstream of those compounds. 3. iamh1 iamh2 did not suppress sur2, so IAM-to-IAA conversion is not required for sur2 auxin overproduction.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Hypocotyl and shoot tissues highlighted by IAM responses
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls elongation growth and developmental responses.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not supported as endogenous auxin-biosynthetic targets; more relevant to defense/camalexin engineering. This study warns against assigning them to IAOx-to-IAA conversion without whole-family genetics.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Previously proposed to act in IAOx conversion toward downstream indolic products; literature suggests CYP71A12/A13 produce indole-3-cyanohydrin/GS-IAN-related intermediates rather than directly generating IAA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: cyp71a12 cyp71a13 cyp71a18 mutants show no prominent endogenous auxin defects, normal response to exogenous IAOx/IAN/IAM, and no suppression of sur2, indicating dispensability for IAOx-mediated auxin production under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: High-order triple mutant phenotyping; precursor feeding assays; sur2 genetic interaction; metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Previously proposed precursor-forming or flux-diverting step upstream of auxin; not supported for target-product formation here.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Standard laboratory growth; dark/light shifts; exogenous IAOx, IAN, IAM, IAA; sur2 background.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not characterized in this paper; Supplementary single-cell data suggest partial overlap with SUR2 in cortex cells.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch point/side-route from IAOx to other indolic metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted metabolomics by LC-MS/MS; single-cell expression reference without accession.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper notes CYP71A12/A13 are implicated in camalexin-related metabolism and GS-IAN/indole-3-cyanohydrin formation, but not in IAOx-derived auxin biosynthesis. Role of CYP71A18 in camalexin remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: IAOx branch linked to defense metabolism, not established auxin branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely impacts defense metabolites such as camalexin/related nitrile intermediates rather than growth under standard conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Not established for auxin pathway here; likely indole-3-cyanohydrin/defense-related intermediates from prior literature
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established for IAA biosynthesis; evidence argues against major control of IAOx-derived IAA production.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum/microsomal P450 (expected; not directly tested here)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Triple CYP71A knockout plants lacked prominent auxin-deficient phenotypes. 2. They remained sensitive to exogenous IAOx, IAN, and IAM and did not suppress sur2. 3. Therefore, CYP71A12/13/18 are dispensable for the hypothesized IAOx-to-IAA route under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seedling roots and shoots via auxin-associated growth responses
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Growth regulation; in broader pathway context competes with defense metabolism.
